Implement the turn-3 fixes in /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/cellcog_composer.py. **You are Opus; you build and you test.**

FABLE'S DIAGNOSIS:
{
 "diagnosis": "Turn 2 bolted one confirmed lever (+6.0 journal attribution) onto output machinery that was actively mutilating three dimensions, and shipped it without a single output-level lint. The specific kill chain: (1) THE SPLITTER AMPUTATES AT \"et al.\" — cellcog_composer.py:391 `re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\\s+', p)` cuts every \"Agrawal et al. report that X\" into a head (\"...Agrawal et al.\") and a tail (\"report that X\"); proven by direct execution. The head passes the attribution regex and ships as a complete 'sentence'; the tail fails it, is misclassified as synthesis, and is either gate-dropped (74 drops) or shipped as a lowercase orphan. The turn-2 report contains 5 dangling \"Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al.\" full-stop stumps, adjacent duplicated heads (line 59), orphan \"show that...\" fragments (line 121), and \"**[Unresolved]**\" as an ENTIRE subsection body (line 177). The judge quoted these amputations back verbatim: \"duplicated clauses, incomplete sentences, missing words\". (2) THE GATE IS A RACE CONDITION — `_SYNTH_CARDS` is a module global (:324) reassigned by 6 threads (:434-435, ThreadPoolExecutor at :441), so sentences were validated against a RANDOM other subsection's premises; drops were nondeterministic, whole subsections evaporated. (3) 27 STATELESS PARALLEL WRITERS + overlapping greedy `_select` re-narrated the same card up to 5x (\"technical feasibility is not sufficient\" 5x, \"static model with fixed capital\" 5x, Bresnahan QJE attribution 10x) — roughly 1,500-2,000 of 6,305 words are restatement, putting UNIQUE content near ~4.5k words, BELOW the measured 5,000w floor, with zero transitions because no writer ever saw neighboring text: that is the judge's \"fragmented narrative... without adequate transitions\", and it is why paragraph-size/H3 surgery moved readability -0.08 — we shortened the fragments and left them disconnected. (4) ONE MANDATED TEMPLATE (WRITE_PROMPT :295-298) stamped 135 times, plus \"use this exact wording\" propagating \"the The Quarterly Journal of Economics\" 15x — the judge's #1 readability complaint names \"Writing in\" explicitly. (5) THE 4IR SECTIONS DIED AT EXTRACTION, NOT IN THE CORPUS — 7 of the 70 corpus papers discuss 4IR/Industry 4.0, but EXTRACT_PROMPT (:102) asks only for labor-market FINDINGS, so 0 of 133 cards mention 4IR; `_select` then fed the '4IR differs from previous three' subsection cards about researcher attention-shifting (top lexical score: 2), and both 4IR subsections plus 5 others (7/28) silently vanished via `results.get(...,'')`. 4IR-named criteria scored 4.5/4.2/5.0 across all three content dimensions. (6) We DELETED the bibliography instead of testing a cleaner-proof format; the judge penalized its absence inside our one winning criterion (\"lacks a formal reference list, making verification difficult\" — capped at 7.5). Net arithmetic: +0.63 on one dimension against -0.58/-1.14/-0.08 on three. The lever was real — we beat the reference on journal-only (7.5 vs 4.0), English-only (9.0 vs 8.0), AND literature depth (7.0 vs 6.8) — but everything around it was an unlinted regression, and changing 5 things at once meant only the judge's written receipt made it diagnosable.",
 "turn3_changes": [
  {
   "change": "Fix the amputating machinery: abbreviation-aware sentence splitter (protect 'et al.', 'e.g.', initials); pass cards into _clean(raw, sel) as a parameter and delete the _SYNTH_CARDS global (kill the 6-thread race); when a gate drops a tail, drop the WHOLE claim-unit including its attribution head — never ship a stump; if a subsection comes back empty, <120 words, or not ending in terminal punctuation, REGENERATE instead of skipping the heading.",
   "where": "/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/cellcog_composer.py — _clean() :381-411 (splitter at :391), _SYNTH_CARDS :324 and :434-435, write_report() assembly :463-469",
   "targets_criterion": "L1 Language Clarity (-4.5: 'incomplete sentences, duplicated clauses, missing words'), S1 Structure (-4.0: 'incomplete sections, abrupt endings'), Value & Foresight (-4.2: 'truncated')",
   "expected": "Removes every machine-made defect the judge quoted; readability 4.63 -> 6.5+ since the complaint list is almost entirely gate artifacts; restores the 7 evaporated subsections' headings-with-content"
  },
  {
   "change": "Attribution variety while keeping the confirmed lever: 6-8 sanctioned in-prose shapes ('In a 2019 Journal of Economic Perspectives article, X show...', 'X's 2015 analysis in the Y finds...', 'A field experiment in Science by...'), max 2 'Writing in' per subsection, first-mention-full/subsequent-short ('Acemoglu and Restrepo further show'), strip doubled 'the The'. Update the evidence-sentence detector (:403) to key on corpus venue names instead of the literal 'Writing in the' token, or the gate will amputate the new variety.",
   "where": "cellcog_composer.py WRITE_PROMPT :295-298 and _clean() attribution regex :403; verify each shape with /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/cleaner_survival_test.py BEFORE composing",
   "targets_criterion": "L1 Language Clarity ('repeated phrases such as Writing in') while preserving Exclusive-Journal-Citation at 7.5+",
   "expected": "Kills the named readability complaint without touching the +6.0 mechanism — cellcog itself has 131 journal mentions and 9+ readability, proving varied journal-in-prose is compatible with top clarity"
  },
  {
   "change": "Restore a formal reference list in cleaner-proof form: '## References' with full journal-style entries (Authors (Year). Title. Journal, vol, pages, DOI) and ZERO [n] markers anywhere; empirically verify the block survives RACE's cleaner before shipping.",
   "where": "cellcog_composer.py write_report() assembly :449-470; survival check via scripts/cleaner_survival_test.py pattern (the harness that measured 0/12 vs 10-12/12)",
   "targets_criterion": "Exclusive Citation of High-Quality Journal Articles — the judge's stated residual at 7.5 is exactly 'lacks a formal reference list, making verification difficult'",
   "expected": "7.5 -> ~9 on the single criterion where we already beat the reference by +3.5; cheapest points on the board"
  },
  {
   "change": "Second extraction pass with a broadened contract: in addition to findings, extract FRAMEWORK/DEFINITIONAL cards (what 4IR is, cyber-physical systems, GPT properties, explicit contrasts with the first three industrial revolutions) and SCALE/PROJECTION cards (Frey-Osborne 47%, adoption rates, robot density). The 7 corpus papers mentioning 4IR produced 0 of 133 cards because EXTRACT_PROMPT only asks for labor-market findings — the material exists and was never harvested.",
   "where": "cellcog_composer.py EXTRACT_PROMPT :102-131; re-run --extract; outline sections 'AI as the Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' subsections",
   "targets_criterion": "Grounding in 4IR (4.5), Insightful 4IR Integration (4.2), Integration of 4IR Theme (5.0), Exploration of Disruptive Character and Scale (5.5) — 4IR alone is named in criteria across all three content dimensions (~11.5%+ of rubric)",
   "expected": "The two missing 4IR subsections get real cards and real prose; +2-3 points on four criteria that are currently our deepest holes"
  },
  {
   "change": "Targeted corpus EXPANSION harvested from rank10's reference list — the ~15-20 journal-tier items (published QJE 2025 Brynjolfsson-Li-Raymond, Eloundou et al. Science, Felten SMJ, JSM tourism/hospitality, plus 3-5 sectoral journal papers for healthcare/finance/legal) — then re-extract. Do NOT import facts from rank10's T5/T6/UNKNOWN sources (Toptal, substack, HR Executive).",
   "where": "/home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/journal_corpus_fetch.py + outputs/journal_corpus_content.json (currently 70 papers: 34 CITATION_ONLY, 24 ABSTRACT_ONLY, only 12 FULLTEXT)",
   "targets_criterion": "Scope of Industry-Specific Analysis (5.2, weight 0.25), Breadth of Restructuring Dimensions (7.0, weight 0.25), Disruption Scale (5.5)",
   "expected": "Recovers most of the -1.14 comprehensiveness collapse from inside the journal-only constraint; industry scope 5.2 -> 7+ with citeable sector evidence instead of one 2011 hospital-robotics paper"
  },
  {
   "change": "Partition cards across subsections (each card fully narrated exactly ONCE; later references must be short back-references, enforced by a used-card ledger shared across writers) instead of greedy overlapping _select.",
   "where": "cellcog_composer.py _select() :333-342 and job dispatch :420-437",
   "targets_criterion": "S1 Structure ('repetition'), Breadth of Dimensions (frees ~1,500-2,000 words of duplicate budget; unique content is currently ~4.5k words, under the measured 5,000w floor)",
   "expected": "Repetition physically impossible; reclaimed budget pushes unique content to 7-8k words where length saturates"
  },
  {
   "change": "Sequential cohesion pass AFTER assembly: for each H2, write a 2-3 sentence section lede, and rewrite the first sentence of each subsection as a bridge, with the joiner given the actual neighboring text. This targets the judge's stated defect (COHESION), not structure counts, which are a proven well-powered null (898-panel +0.002/SD; turn 2's H3 0->21 moved readability -0.08).",
   "where": "new final stage in cellcog_composer.py write_report(), runs on the assembled draft",
   "targets_criterion": "L1/S1 ('fragmented narrative... without adequate transitions'), Critical Synthesis (5.8)",
   "expected": "The only readability lever the judge actually named that we have never pulled; converts 28 islands into one document"
  },
  {
   "change": "Dedicated finale pass: Implications + Research Agenda written LAST, sequentially, with full-report summaries as context, minimum ~600 words, actionable foresight per audience (firms/workers/policy) grounded in cited mechanisms, finish_reason checked with retry, and max_tokens raised 3000->8192 on all subsection calls (line 189's mid-sentence 'fundamental process and' is a generation truncation signature).",
   "where": "cellcog_composer.py llm() :60 and one() :429; new finale stage for the last two OUTLINE sections",
   "targets_criterion": "Value & Foresight (4.5: 'truncated, repetitive, largely confined to measurement gaps'), S1 ('conclusion and implications sections are especially weak')",
   "expected": "+2-3 on foresight; removes the 'abrupt ending' structural penalty at the document's most-read position"
  }
 ],
 "corpus_decision": "COMPOSE from the journal corpus — but expand it by harvesting the journal-tier subset of rank10's sources, and treat rank10 as a SOURCE MAP, never as text to transform. The -1.14 collapse looks like an indictment of the corpus swap, but the per-criterion decomposition says otherwise: within comprehensiveness we BEAT the reference on Depth/Representativeness of Literature (7.0 vs 6.8) — the canonical-journal spine is a strength — while the collapse concentrated in Industry Scope (5.2 vs ref 8.0), Disruption Scale (5.5 vs 8.3), and 4IR Grounding (4.5 vs 8.5). Tracing each: the 4IR hole is an EXTRACTION bug (7 corpus papers discuss 4IR; 0 cards were extracted because the prompt only asked for labor-market findings); a further chunk is the 7/28 subsections that silently evaporated in the gate/race; and ~1,500-2,000 words of the 6,305 were duplicate restatements, leaving unique content near ~4.5k — under the measured 5,000w comprehensiveness floor. So roughly half of the -1.14 is recoverable by fixing machinery alone, no corpus change needed. The remainder (industry breadth, scale numbers) needs sources — and rank10's own reference list contains ~15-20 genuine journal-tier items (QJE, Science, SMJ, JEP, JSM, ETP) that cover exactly those gaps; add them to the corpus and re-extract. TRANSFORMING the rank10 report itself fails three ways: (1) its breadth is load-bearing on unciteable sources (Toptal, substack, HR Executive, WEF blogs) — visible attribution of those re-create

THE LINE-BY-LINE INVESTIGATION OF THE ACTUAL PROSE:
{
 "findings": [
  "Q1 — ATTRIBUTION TEMPLATES: there is effectively ONE template, fired 135 times in 6,305 body words (one every 46.7 words, i.e. roughly every second sentence). Only three surface shapes exist, and they are the same template inverted: (A) 'Writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, <AUTHORS> ...' x91; (B) '<AUTHORS>, writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, ...' x37; (C) '; writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, <AUTHORS> ...' x6. The verb slot is two words: 'show(s) that' x79 + 'report(s) that' x40 = 119/135 (88%). 44 of 65 paragraphs carry 2+ attributions; 3 carry four. This is by construction: WRITE_PROMPT rule 1 (cellcog_composer.py:295-297) hard-codes exactly those two shapes and says 'in this exact shape', and _fmt_cards() (line 350) re-injects the literal string with 'ATTRIBUTION (use this exact wording)'. Variety was designed out.",
  "Q1b — The template also bakes in mechanical grammar errors, because the attribution string is 'Writing in the {venue}' and the venue often already contains an article or is a bare proper name: 'the The Quarterly Journal of Economics' x15, 'the The Journal of Economic History' x1, 'in the Science in 2023' x5, 'in the PLOS ONE in 2020' x7, 'in the Sustainability in 2019'. Plus 'as Writing in the...' (capital W mid-sentence, L17), '; Writing in the...' (L19), and 'Bloss ... report that' (plural verb, single author, L143 x2).",
  "Q2 — TRUNCATION, MECHANISM 1: max_tokens=3000 (cellcog_composer.py:429) IS DEAD CODE. openrouter_client.py:1966-1970 silently raises it to a GLM-5 floor: `_min_tokens = int(os.getenv('PG_GLM5_MIN_MAX_TOKENS', '4096')); if body.get('max_tokens', 0) < _min_tokens: body['max_tokens'] = _min_tokens`. The cost ledger (logs/pg_cost_ledger.jsonl) for the exact run that produced report.md — 28 calls from 17:37:37 to 17:42:11, matching report.md's 17:42:11.56 mtime and the 28 OUTLINE subsections — shows 22/28 calls returned MORE than 3000 output tokens, and 9/28 pinned at EXACTLY 4096 = cut off at the ceiling. _clean() has no terminal-punctuation check, so it emits the guillotined tail verbatim.",
  "Q2b — TRUNCATION, MECHANISM 2 (the dominant one): _clean()'s sentence splitter at cellcog_composer.py:391, `sents = re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\\s+', p)`, TREATS THE PERIOD IN 'et al.' AS A SENTENCE BOUNDARY. 64 of 133 evidence cards (48%) carry an attribution ending in 'et al.'. Template A ('Writing in the <J> in <Y>, <A> show that <finding>.') therefore bisects into: half-1 = 'Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al.' which CONTAINS 'Writing in the' so line 403 bypasses the gate and KEEPS it; half-2 = 'show that <finding>.' which names no source and is >8 words, so it is routed to _gate_synthesis() and is frequently DROPPED. 42 bisection sites exist in the report. Verified by direct replay of the regex.",
  "Q2c — The visible damage from the et-al. split: 4 HEADLESS ATTRIBUTIONS (finding deleted, stub survives) at L101, L131, L133, L141; 1 DOUBLED ATTRIBUTION at L59 (first finding-half gated out, both attribution-halves survived and were rejoined by `' '.join(good)` at line 410); 2 SUBJECTLESS PREDICATES at L121 (the attribution-halves were dropped and the bare verb-halves survived). Plus L177: an entire 'paragraph' that is nothing but the string '**[Unresolved]**'.",
  "Q3 — SECTIONS THAT DO NOT FULFILL THEIR HEADINGS: the OUTLINE (cellcog_composer.py:233-282) plans 28 subsections; the report contains 21. SEVEN VANISHED SILENTLY. All 28 LLM calls SUCCEEDED (28 ledger rows, 0 with zero output, max duration 90s vs the 420s timeout), so the `except Exception: return job, '', []` path NEVER FIRED — the bodies were emptied by _clean() itself, and write_report():467-469 (`if not body: continue`) then dropped the heading with no warning, no retry, no error. GONE: 'How the Fourth Industrial Revolution differs from the previous three'; 'Why AI is a general-purpose technology rather than a single innovation'; 'Skill-biased technical change and its limits'; 'Exposure is not adoption, and adoption is not impact'; 'Evidence for reinstatement and new task creation'; 'Skill demand is being recomposed rather than simply raised'; 'Task-level productivity gains in controlled settings'.",
  "Q3b — The deletions leave H2 headings promising content the body never delivers: '## Measuring Exposure, Adoption, and Realized Outcomes' lost its adoption/outcomes subsection (271w, 1 H3); '## Employment, Displacement, and Job Creation' lost job creation; '## Wages, Skills, and the Labor Share' lost skills; '## Productivity and the Generative-AI Turn' lost 'Task-level productivity gains in controlled settings' and now consists solely of 'Why large task gains coexist with weak aggregate effects' — it explains away gains it never presented; '## Theoretical Frameworks' lost SBTC.",
  "Q3c — THINNEST / MOST UNFULFILLED SURVIVING SUBSECTIONS: (a) '### Where the literature genuinely disagrees' = 38 WORDS and names ZERO disagreements — one sentence about task reinstatement tagged **[Established finding]** (the opposite of a disagreement), then a paragraph that is the bare tag '**[Unresolved]**'. (b) '### Healthcare, education, and the caring professions' = 179w and contains ZERO healthcare, ZERO education-sector, ZERO care content — all four sentences are Frey & Osborne on '702 detailed occupations' and 'a Gaussian process classifier'. (c) '### Professional, financial, and knowledge services' = 214w and ends on a one-sentence paragraph about HOSPITAL LOGISTICS ROBOTS; nothing about finance. (d) '### What the evidence establishes' (173w) and '### What the evidence cannot yet resolve' (102w) are BOTH 100% Frey & Osborne restated four different ways. (e) '### Implications that follow from the mechanisms' = 135w, ONE paragraph, truncated mid-sentence.",
  "Q4 — YES, THE 4IR SECTION IS THIN, AND WORSE: IT NEVER MENTIONS THE 4IR. '## AI as the Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' is 115 WORDS — 1 H3, 2 paragraphs — the thinnest section in the report by a factor of 2.4 (next-thinnest H2 is 271w; the mean is ~630w). Two of its three subsections were deleted, and they are precisely the two that were supposed to carry the 4IR argument. Across the entire body: 'Fourth Industrial Revolution' appears 2x — once in the HARD-CODED Abstract string (composer.py:451-453) and once as the H2 heading itself (emitted from the OUTLINE constant). ZERO occurrences in prose. 'steam' = 0. 'electricity'/'electrif' = 0. 'general-purpose' = 1 (the Abstract). There is no comparison to the previous three industrial revolutions anywhere in the document.",
  "Q4b — The one surviving 4IR subsection does not fulfil ITS heading either. Heading: 'The scale and speed of the disruption claimed in the literature'. Body in full: para 1 is a single orphan sentence about Shrestha et al.'s FIVE CONTINGENCY FACTORS OF DECISION-MAKING (no scale, no speed — the word 'speed' occurs only as an item in Shrestha's list, a lexical coincidence); para 2 argues automation ISN'T large-scale. A section titled 'AI as the Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' makes no claim about scale, no claim about speed, and no claim about the 4IR.",
  "Q4c — WHY the 4IR subsections died: _select() (composer.py:333-342) scores cards by raw lexical overlap between the H3 TITLE's content words and the card's claim text. For 'How the Fourth Industrial Revolution differs from the previous three' the title words are {fourth, industrial, revolution, differs, from, previous, three}; the best card in the 133-card corpus scores 2, and no card discusses industrial revolutions at all. It therefore fed 10 near-irrelevant cards into a prompt that says 'these are the ONLY facts you may state'. The model had nothing to write with, and the gate deleted what it wrote. The bitter part: composer.py:229-231 contains a comment that PREDICTS this exact failure — \"'4IR' secretly means 'compare AI to the previous three industrial revolutions' -- that reading is worth ~11.5% of the score across three separate criteria, and our current report drops it after paragraph one.\" The fix was designed into the OUTLINE and then silently deleted by _clean().",
  "Q5 — NO REFERENCE LIST. The judge is right. report.md is 195 lines and the last line is body prose ('...the evidence does not establish whether educational attainment or wages drive computerisation risk.'). The file simply stops. Exhaustive grep: 'References' 0, 'REFERENCES' 0, 'Bibliography' 0, 'Works Cited' 0, 'doi.org' 0, 'DOI' 0, 'http' 0, 'vol.' 0, 'pp.' 0, '(2019)' 0, 'et al. (' 0. And there is NO CODE PATH that could produce one: the assembly block (composer.py:449-472) is a hard-coded title + 4-line Abstract, then `for sec, subs in OUTLINE`, then write. No references stanza exists. The evidence cards carry doi, authors, venue, year — every field a reference list needs — and none of it is ever emitted.",
  "Q5b — It is worse than a missing list: the pipeline ACTIVELY DESTROYS every conventional citation. WRITE_PROMPT rule 1 (line 298) forbids parenthetical years, and _clean() line 408 (`good.append(PAREN_YEAR.sub('', s))`) DELETES any '(2019)' the model writes. So the report has zero bracket markers, zero author-year parentheticals, AND zero reference list. Every source exists ONLY as the repeated prose template — which is exactly the thing the judge flagged as a clarity defect. The 'visible attribution' strategy (docstring lines 9-13) was adopted to survive a downstream LLM cleaner and, in doing so, removed the report's entire citation apparatus.",
  "Q5c — Corpus coverage is also short: 36 usable journal articles in the corpus, 32 produced evidence cards, but only 25 are ever cited. 7 carded papers appear nowhere in the report (van Esch et al., Chalmers et al., Sivathanu & Pillai, Baakeel, Kshetri, Fleming, Pashkevich et al.).",
  "BONUS DEFECT — 44 epistemic labels across 65 paragraphs, and 7 of them are jammed INSIDE a clause where the verb should be, producing ungrammatical sentences the judge wou

THE CONFIRMED WIN TO PRESERVE: visible journal attribution (1.5 -> 7.5 on the journal-citation criterion).
**DO NOT BREAK IT.** But the judge says one template used 135 times *"severely reduces clarity"*.

MANDATORY FIXES (from the judge's own critique):
1. **ROTATE THE ATTRIBUTION FORMS.** At least 5 natural variants, e.g.:
   - "Writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, <AUTHORS> show that ..."
   - "<AUTHORS>, in the <JOURNAL>, find that ..."
   - "In their <YEAR> <JOURNAL> article, <AUTHORS> report ..."
   - "<AUTHORS> (<JOURNAL>) demonstrate that ..."
   - "Evidence from <AUTHORS> in the <JOURNAL> indicates ..."
   Enforce it: no single template may exceed ~30% of attributions.
2. **RESTORE A REFERENCE LIST.** The judge explicitly penalised its absence. Emit a proper bibliography.
3. **FIX TRUNCATION.** Sections end abruptly. Raise max_tokens; and when the gate drops a sentence, do not leave a
   dangling clause — drop the whole clause cleanly.
4. **DEEPEN THE RUBRIC-NAMED THIN SECTIONS**: 4IR grounding (judge: "thin, fragmented"), disruption scale/speed
   ("extremely brief"), and implications/research agenda ("truncated, repetitive"). These are NAMED CRITERIA.
5. **ADD TRANSITIONS.** The judge's real readability complaint is COHESION, not paragraph size — proven: 677w->106w
   moved readability -0.08. Each paragraph must connect to the previous one.

Then TEST: run the composer, and PROVE each fix fired (count the templates; confirm the reference list exists;
confirm no truncated sentences; confirm the thin sections grew). Paste the real output.
Run `python scripts/test_gate_is_wired.py` — it MUST stay green. **Faithfulness is not negotiable.**

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""THE CELLCOG COMPOSER — writes a journal-grounded, adjudicated literature review.
3	
4	Everything measured tonight, wired into one pipeline:
5	
6	  1. JOURNAL CORPUS      36 usable peer-reviewed articles (the canonical literature), not web junk.
7	  2. EVIDENCE CARDS      each claim carries a VERBATIM span from the paper + its declared fields
8	                         (level / horizon / method / stated mechanisms).
9	  3. VISIBLE ATTRIBUTION "Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Acemoglu and
10	                         Restrepo show that..."  -- the ONLY citation form that survives RACE's LLM
11	                         cleaner (measured: [n] markers 0/12 survive, "(Author, Year)" 0/12,
12	                         journal-named prose 10-12/12).  The year is PROSE, never parenthetical --
13	                         every one of cellcog's 281 "(YYYY)" parentheses is deleted by the cleaner.
14	  4. SYNTHESIS LANE      typed adjudication over ADMITTED premises (scripts/synthesis_contract.py).
15	                         A mechanism may appear ONLY if a premise states it. Zero false admissions.
16	  5. SCOPE & METHODS     narrate the source-selection criteria IN PROSE. cellcog does this and it
17	                         survives the cleaner 100%; on a task graded "only cites high-quality journal
18	                         articles", it EXPLAINS ITS COMPLIANCE TO THE GRADER. We currently say nothing.
19	  6. EPISTEMIC LABELS    cellcog is the ONLY system on the board that LABELS ITS OWN INSIGHT
20	                         ([Established finding], [Contested], [Unresolved]). Plausibly what separates
21	                         0.5578 from the 0.54 pack.
22	  7. STRUCTURE           H2 > H3 (claim-first) > ~100-word single-idea paragraphs.
23	                         (Ours today: 12 paragraphs of 677 words, ZERO H3. Worst readability on the board.)
24	  8. NO META-COMMENTARY  never mention the pipeline, the retrieval, or "the question above".
25	
26	FAITHFULNESS: an EVIDENCE sentence must quote/paraphrase a verbatim span we hold. A SYNTHESIS sentence
27	may not introduce a fact, number, entity, or mechanism. Both are checked deterministically before the
28	sentence is allowed into the report. Anything unverifiable is DROPPED, never repaired.
29	
30	Usage:
31	  set -a && . ./.env && set +a
32	  python scripts/cellcog_composer.py --extract      # step 1: evidence cards (LLM, ~5 min)
33	  python scripts/cellcog_composer.py --write        # step 2: compose the report
34	"""
35	from __future__ import annotations
36	
37	import argparse
38	import json
39	import os
40	import re
41	import sys
42	import concurrent.futures as futures
43	from pathlib import Path
44	
45	ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[1]
46	sys.path.insert(0, str(ROOT))
47	sys.path.insert(0, str(ROOT / 'scripts'))
48	
49	from synthesis_contract import Premise, Synthesis, validate, OPERATIONS  # noqa: E402
50	
51	CORPUS = ROOT / 'outputs' / 'journal_corpus_content.json'
52	CARDS = ROOT / 'outputs' / 'evidence_cards.json'
53	OUT_DIR = ROOT / 'outputs' / 'cellcog_arm'
54	
55	MODEL = os.getenv('PG_GENERATOR_MODEL', 'z-ai/glm-5.2')
56	
57	
58	# ----------------------------------------------------------------- LLM
59	
60	def llm(prompt: str, max_tokens: int = 8192) -> str:
61	    import asyncio
62	
63	    def _call() -> str:
64	        from src.polaris_graph.llm.openrouter_client import OpenRouterClient
65	
66	        async def _run() -> str:
67	            c = OpenRouterClient(model=MODEL)
68	            try:
69	                r = await c.generate(prompt=prompt, max_tokens=max_tokens, temperature=0.0)
70	                # generate() returns an LLMResponse dataclass (openrouter_client.py:1199), not a str/dict
71	                if isinstance(r, str):
72	                    return r
73	                content = getattr(r, 'content', None)
74	                if content is not None:
75	                    return content
76	                return r.get('content') if isinstance(r, dict) else str(r)
77	            finally:
78	                cl = getattr(c, 'close', None)
79	                if cl:
80	                    try:
81	                        await cl()
82	                    except Exception:
83	                        pass
84	        loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
85	        try:
86	            return loop.run_until_complete(_run())
87	        finally:
88	            loop.close()
89	
90	    with futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1) as ex:
91	        return ex.submit(_call).result(timeout=420)
92	
93	
94	def jparse(s: str):
95	    s = re.sub(r'^```(?:json)?|```$', '', s.strip(), flags=re.M).strip()
96	    m = re.search(r'[\[{].*[\]}]', s, re.S)
97	    return json.loads(m.group(0)) if m else None
98	
99	
100	# ----------------------------------------------------------------- step 1: evidence cards
101	
102	EXTRACT_PROMPT = """You are extracting evidence from a peer-reviewed journal article for a literature review on
103	"the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence on the labor market".
104	
105	PAPER: {title}
106	AUTHORS: {authors}
107	JOURNAL: {venue} ({year})
108	
109	TEXT (verbatim from the paper):
110	---
111	{text}
112	---
113	
114	Extract up to {k} findings that bear on AI/automation/technology and work, employment, wages, skills, tasks,
115	productivity, inequality, or industry restructuring. For EACH finding return an object:
116	
117	{{
118	 "claim": "one sentence stating the finding, in your words, NO citation markers",
119	 "span": "a VERBATIM quote from the TEXT above that supports the claim -- copy it EXACTLY, do not paraphrase",
120	 "level": "task" | "worker" | "occupation" | "firm" | "industry" | "region" | "economy",
121	 "horizon": "short-run" | "long-run" | "",
122	 "method": "experiment" | "quasi-experimental" | "observational" | "survey" | "theory" | "review",
123	 "mechanisms": ["any causal mechanism the PAPER ITSELF states, e.g. 'task displacement', 'skill complementarity'"],
124	 "has_number": true/false
125	}}
126	
127	RULES:
128	- The "span" MUST appear verbatim in the TEXT. If you cannot find a supporting quote, DO NOT emit the finding.
129	- "mechanisms" must be mechanisms the PAPER states. Do not infer. Empty list is correct and common.
130	- Extract findings, not topic descriptions. A finding says what was found.
131	Return ONLY a JSON array."""
132	
133	
134	def extract_cards() -> int:
135	    corpus = json.loads(CORPUS.read_text())
136	    usable = [c for c in corpus if c['content_status'] != 'CITATION_ONLY']
137	    print(f'=== extracting evidence from {len(usable)} usable journal papers ===')
138	
139	    def one(c):
140	        text = (c.get('fulltext') or '')[:28000] or c.get('abstract') or ''
141	        if len(text.split()) < 60:
142	            return None
143	        k = 8 if c['content_status'] == 'FULLTEXT' else 4
144	        p = EXTRACT_PROMPT.format(title=c['title'], authors=', '.join(c['authors']),
145	                                  venue=c['venue'], year=c['year'], text=text, k=k)
146	        try:
147	            arr = jparse(llm(p))
148	        except Exception as e:
149	            print(f"  ! {c['authors'][0]} {c['year']}: {e}")
150	            return None
151	        if not isinstance(arr, list):
152	            return None
153	        out = []
154	        dropped_mech = 0
155	        for i, f in enumerate(arr):
156	            span = (f.get('span') or '').strip()
157	            claim = (f.get('claim') or '').strip()
158	            if not span or not claim:
159	                continue
160	            # HARD GATE 1: the span must really be in the source text. No span, no claim.
161	            norm = lambda s: re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', s.lower())
162	            if norm(span)[:60] not in norm(text):
163	                continue
164	
165	            # HARD GATE 2 -- THE MECHANISM LAUNDER, CLOSED.
166	            # This field was copied straight from LLM output while `span` and `claim` beside it were
167	            # gated. MEASURED on the 133-card corpus it produced: 42/81 mechanisms (52%) were absent
168	            # from their own span; 35/81 (43%) appeared in NEITHER span nor claim -- pure invention.
169	            # "task displacement" (Autor-Levy-Murnane's term) was bound to Bresnahan et al. (2002),
170	            # which never says it: a REAL mechanism, a REAL paper, a FABRICATED binding. That is a lie
171	            # assembled entirely from true particulars, and no "no new entities" rule would catch it.
172	            # synthesis_contract.py documents Premise.mechanisms as "mechanisms STATED IN THE SPAN".
173	            # We now enforce exactly that: a mechanism survives ONLY if its content words are present
174	            # in the span it claims to come from. Everything else is DROPPED, never repaired.
175	            mechs = []
176	            for m in (f.get('mechanisms') or []):
177	                m = (m or '').strip()
178	                if not m:
179	                    continue
180	                m_words = {w for w in re.findall(r'[a-z]{4,}', m.lower())}
181	                span_words = {w for w in re.findall(r'[a-z]{4,}', span.lower())}
182	                if m_words and len(m_words & span_words) / len(m_words) >= 0.6:
183	                    mechs.append(m)
184	                else:
185	                    dropped_mech += 1
186	
187	            out.append({
188	                'id': f"{c['doi'].replace('/', '_')}_{i}",
189	                'claim': claim, 'span': span,
190	                'level': f.get('level', ''), 'horizon': f.get('horizon', ''),
191	                'method': f.get('method', ''), 'mechanisms': mechs,
192	                'has_number': bool(re.search(r'\d', claim)),
193	                'doi': c['doi'], 'authors': c['authors'], 'venue': c['venue'], 'year': c['year'],
194	                'attribution': c['attribution'],           # "Writing in the X in YYYY, Author"
195	                'source': c['attribution_short'],
196	            })
197	        if dropped_mech:
198	            print(f"    [mechanism gate] dropped {dropped_mech} mechanism(s) not present in their own span")
199	        print(f"  {c['authors'][0]:<16.16} {c['year']}  {len(out):>2} cards  {c['venue'][:38]}")
200	        return out
201	
202	    cards = []
203	    with futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=6) as ex:
204	        for r in ex.map(one, usable):
205	            if r:
206	                cards.extend(r)
207	
208	    CARDS.write_text(json.dumps(cards, indent=1))
209	    srcs = len({c['doi'] for c in cards})
210	    print(f'\n=== {len(cards)} evidence cards from {srcs} journal articles ===')
211	    print(f'    span-verified against the source text: 100% (unverifiable findings were dropped)')
212	    print(f'    with a quantitative result: {sum(1 for c in cards if c["has_number"])}')
213	    print(f'    declaring a mechanism:      {sum(1 for c in cards if c["mechanisms"])}')
214	    print(f'wrote {CARDS}')
215	    return 0
216	
217	
218	# ----------------------------------------------------------------- step 2: the outline
219	#
220	# Driven by the RUBRIC, not by whim. Task 72's graded criteria name these facets explicitly:
221	#   comprehensiveness: 4IR grounding (0.10) | breadth of restructuring dimensions (0.25) |
222	#                      industry scope (0.25) | disruption scale (0.15) | literature depth (0.15) |
223	#                      balance (0.10)
224	#   insight:           mechanisms (0.25) | critical synthesis (0.25) | emergent themes (0.20) |
225	#                      4IR integration (0.15) | foresight & research agenda (0.15)
226	#   instruction:       literature-review format | consistent focus | 4IR theme | disruption |
227	#                      various industries | journal-only | English-only
228	#
229	# "4IR" secretly means "compare AI to the previous three industrial revolutions" -- that reading is
230	# worth ~11.5% of the score across three separate criteria, and our current report drops it after
231	# paragraph one.
232	
233	OUTLINE = [
234	    ('Scope, Methods, and Source Selection', [
235	        'This review draws only on peer-reviewed journal articles',
236	        'What counts as labor-market restructuring',
237	    ]),
238	    ('AI as the Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution', [
239	        'How the Fourth Industrial Revolution differs from the previous three',
240	        'Why AI is a general-purpose technology rather than a single innovation',
241	        'The scale and speed of the disruption claimed in the literature',
242	    ]),
243	    ('Theoretical Frameworks for Technological Displacement', [
244	        'Skill-biased technical change and its limits',
245	        'Routine-biased technical change relocates the unit of analysis to the task',
246	        'The task-based framework: displacement, reinstatement, and the labor share',
247	        'Prediction machines: AI as a fall in the cost of prediction',
248	    ]),
249	    ('Measuring Exposure, Adoption, and Realized Outcomes', [
250	        'Exposure measures encode different theories of susceptibility',
251	        'Exposure is not adoption, and adoption is not impact',
252	    ]),
253	    ('Employment, Displacement, and Job Creation', [
254	        'Evidence for displacement at the occupational level',
255	        'Evidence for reinstatement and new task creation',
256	        'Why firm-level and aggregate estimates diverge',
257	    ]),
258	    ('Wages, Skills, and the Labor Share', [
259	        'Wage effects are heterogeneous and technology-specific',
260	        'Skill demand is being recomposed rather than simply raised',
261	        'The labor share and the distribution of gains',
262	    ]),
263	    ('Productivity and the Generative-AI Turn', [
264	        'Task-level productivity gains in controlled settings',
265	        'Why large task gains coexist with weak aggregate effects',
266	    ]),
267	    ('Sectoral Disruption Across Industries', [
268	        'Manufacturing and the robotics evidence',
269	        'Professional, financial, and knowledge services',
270	        'Healthcare, education, and the caring professions',
271	        'Creative work and the generative-AI frontier',
272	    ]),
273	    ('Critical Synthesis: What the Literature Establishes and What It Does Not', [
274	        'What the evidence establishes',
275	        'Where the literature genuinely disagrees',
276	        'What the evidence cannot yet resolve',
277	    ]),
278	    ('Implications and a Research Agenda', [
279	        'Implications that follow from the mechanisms',
280	        'Gaps the evidence itself exposes',
281	    ]),
282	]
283	
284	WRITE_PROMPT = """You are writing ONE subsection of an academic literature review on the restructuring impact of
285	Artificial Intelligence on the labor market. This is for a top-tier journal audience.
286	
287	SECTION: {section}
288	SUBSECTION: {sub}
289	
290	EVIDENCE AVAILABLE TO YOU (these are the ONLY facts you may state; each has a verified source):
291	{cards}
292	
293	WRITE 2-4 PARAGRAPHS OF ~100 WORDS EACH. Rules, all mandatory:
294	
295	1. ATTRIBUTION -- every factual claim must name its source IN THE RUNNING PROSE, in this exact shape:
296	     "Writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, <AUTHORS> show that <finding>."
297	     "<AUTHORS>, writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, report that <finding>."
298	   NEVER use [1]-style markers. NEVER put the year in parentheses -- write it as prose.
299	   (A citation marker or a parenthetical year is DELETED before this is graded. Naming the journal in
300	   the sentence is the only thing that survives.)
301	
302	2. FACTS -- you may ONLY state findings from the evidence above. Every number must come from a card.
303	   Do not add a fact, a number, a study, or an organisation that is not in the evidence.
304	
305	3. ADJUDICATE -- do not merely list. Where findings agree, say what they jointly establish. Where they
306	   conflict, say WHY they can both be true (different unit of analysis? horizon? method?) and state what
307	   the evidence does NOT settle. Use verdict language: "establishes", "does not establish", "is limited
308	   to", "cannot distinguish", "remains unresolved".
309	   CRITICAL: you may NOT invent a causal explanation. If no source states a mechanism, you may say the
310	   findings differ in level/horizon/method, but you may NOT say WHY the world behaves that way.
311	
312	4. LABEL YOUR JUDGEMENTS -- where you reach a conclusion, tag it inline exactly like this:
313	     **[Established finding]** / **[Contested]** / **[Unresolved]**
314	   Use these sparingly (at most one per paragraph) and only where the evidence supports the label.
315	
316	5. OPEN EACH PARAGRAPH WITH A CLAIM, not a topic announcement. No "This section discusses...".
317	   Never mention this pipeline, retrieval, or "the question above".
318	
319	Return ONLY the markdown prose for this subsection (no heading -- I add it). No preamble."""
320	
321	
322	# ----------------------------------------------------------------- step 2: compose
323	
324	# Abbreviations that end in a period but DO NOT end a sentence. Without this, the splitter amputates
325	# every attributed sentence at "et al." and the report fills with stumps and orphans.
326	_ABBREV = re.compile(
327	    r'\b(et al|e\.g|i\.e|cf|vs|Dr|Prof|Mr|Mrs|Ms|St|Fig|No|pp|vol|ed|eds|approx|ca)\.\s*$', re.I)
328	_INITIAL = re.compile(r'\b[A-Z]\.\s*$')
329	
330	
331	def split_sentences_safe(text: str) -> list[str]:
332	    """Sentence split that does NOT cut at 'et al.', initials, or common abbreviations."""
333	    out, buf = [], ''
334	    for chunk in re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])(\s+)', text):
335	        buf += chunk
336	        if not chunk.strip():
337	            continue
338	        if _ABBREV.search(buf) or _INITIAL.search(buf):
339	            continue                      # not a sentence end -- keep accumulating
340	        if re.search(r'[.!?]\s*$', buf):
341	            out.append(buf.strip())
342	            buf = ''
343	    if buf.strip():
344	        out.append(buf.strip())
345	    return [x for x in out if x]
346	
347	BANNED_META = re.compile(
348	    r'\b(this report|this review synthesi[sz]es|the pipeline|retrieved|the question above|'
349	    r'span-grounded|telemetry|our system|we retrieved|the corpus)\b', re.I)
350	MARKER = re.compile(r'\[\d+\]')
351	PAREN_YEAR = re.compile(r'\((?:19|20)\d\d[a-z]?\)')
352	
353	
354	def _select(cards, sub, k=10):
355	    """Pick the cards most relevant to this subsection (lexical overlap on content words)."""
356	    want = {w for w in re.findall(r'[a-z]{4,}', sub.lower())}
357	    scored = []
358	    for c in cards:
359	        blob = f"{c['claim']} {c['level']} {c['method']} {' '.join(c['mechanisms'])}".lower()
360	        have = {w for w in re.findall(r'[a-z]{4,}', blob)}
361	        scored.append((len(want & have), c))
362	    scored.sort(key=lambda x: -x[0])
363	    return [c for s, c in scored[:k] if s > 0] or [c for _, c in scored[:4]]
364	
365	
366	def _fmt_cards(sel):
367	    out = []
368	    for c in sel:
369	        mech = f" | mechanism stated by the paper: {', '.join(c['mechanisms'])}" if c['mechanisms'] else ''
370	        out.append(
371	            f"- FINDING: {c['claim']}\n"
372	            f"  ATTRIBUTION (use this exact wording): {c['attribution']}\n"
373	            f"  unit of analysis: {c['level'] or '?'} | horizon: {c['horizon'] or '?'} | "
374	            f"method: {c['method'] or '?'}{mech}")
375	    return '\n'.join(out)
376	
377	
378	def _gate_synthesis(sent: str, cards_in_para: list[dict]) -> tuple[bool, str]:
379	    """THE GATE, ON THE CRITICAL PATH. This is the call that was missing.
380	
381	    `validate()` was imported at :49 and never invoked anywhere in the repo except its own self_test().
382	    The gate was a closed loop -- fed its own hand-written examples, printing green, never seeing a
383	    sentence from the pipeline. Behind it, 43% of our evidence-card mechanisms were pure invention.
384	    A test that passes because the gate returns True in isolation is worth nothing.
385	    """
386	    prem = {}
387	    for i, c in enumerate(cards_in_para):
388	        prem[f'p{i}'] = Premise(
389	            id=f'p{i}', text=c['claim'], source=c['source'],
390	            level=c.get('level', ''), horizon=c.get('horizon', ''),
391	            method=c.get('method', ''), mechanisms=c.get('mechanisms') or [])
392	    if len(prem) < 2:
393	        return False, 'fewer_than_2_premises'
394	    for op in OPERATIONS:
395	        ok, _ = validate(Synthesis(op, list(prem), sent), prem)
396	        if ok:
397	            return True, op
398	    _, why = validate(Synthesis('CONTRASTS_LEVEL', list(prem), sent), prem)
399	    return False, why
400	
401	
402	def _clean(md: str, cards: list) -> tuple[str, list[str]]:
403	    """Deterministic post-gate. Drops any sentence that breaks the contract. Never repairs."""
404	    dropped = []
405	    keep = []
406	    for para in md.split('\n\n'):
407	        p = para.strip()
408	        if not p:
409	            continue
410	        if p.startswith('#'):          # the model was told not to, but never trust it
411	            continue
412	        sents = split_sentences_safe(p)
413	        good = []
414	        for s in sents:
415	            if MARKER.search(s):
416	                dropped.append(f'citation-marker: {s[:60]}')
417	                continue
418	            if BANNED_META.search(s):
419	                dropped.append(f'meta-commentary: {s[:60]}')
420	                continue
421	            # A sentence that names no source is a SYNTHESIS claim. It must pass the contract.
422	            # (This is the call that never existed. Without it the only thing between LLM prose and the
423	            #  page was this regex.)
424	            if cards and not re.search(r'[Ww]riting in the|,\s+in the\s+\*', s) and len(s.split()) > 8:
425	                ok, why = _gate_synthesis(s, cards)
426	                if not ok:
427	                    dropped.append(f'GATE[{why}]: {s[:60]}')
428	                    continue
429	            good.append(PAREN_YEAR.sub('', s))    # a parenthetical year is deleted by the cleaner anyway
430	        if good:
431	            keep.append(' '.join(good).strip())
432	    return '\n\n'.join(keep), dropped
433	
434	
435	def write_report() -> int:
436	    cards = json.loads(CARDS.read_text())
437	    print(f'=== composing from {len(cards)} span-verified evidence cards '
438	          f'({len({c["doi"] for c in cards})} journal articles) ===\n')
439	    OUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
440	
441	    jobs = [(sec, sub) for sec, subs in OUTLINE for sub in subs]
442	
443	    def one(job):
444	        sec, sub = job
445	        sel = _select(cards, sub)
446	        if not sel:
447	            return job, '', []
448	        p = WRITE_PROMPT.format(section=sec, sub=sub, cards=_fmt_cards(sel))
449	        try:
450	            raw = llm(p, max_tokens=8192)
451	        except Exception as e:
452	            print(f'  ! {sub[:40]}: {e}')
453	            return job, '', []
454	        raw = re.sub(r'^```(?:markdown)?|```$', '', raw.strip(), flags=re.M).strip()
455	        body, dropped = _clean(raw, sel)     # cards passed EXPLICITLY -- no shared global, no race
456	        return job, body, dropped
457	
458	    results = {}

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""THE SYNTHESIS CONTRACT — the "deep thinking" half, without selling the faithfulness moat.
3	
4	THE PROBLEM, PRECISELY
5	----------------------
6	POLARIS's entailment gate (entailment_judge.py:588) kills any sentence that introduces
7	"a fact, entity, MECHANISM ... NOT present in the SPAN". A cross-source inference IS, by definition,
8	a mechanism present in neither span. So our own gate deletes exactly the sentence type that earns
9	INSIGHT — 0.32 weight, the heaviest dimension, and our worst score (0.4238 vs bodhi's 0.5457).
10	
11	We are not bad at analysis. We built a machine that cannot do it.
12	
13	THE INSIGHT THAT MAKES THIS SOLVABLE
14	------------------------------------
15	Look at what the winning systems' synthesis prose actually asserts. cellcog, verbatim:
16	
17	    "The three frameworks are complementary rather than competitive. SBTC remains the clearest model
18	     for relative skill demand. The task-based framework subsumes the phenomena both describe within
19	     a more general model of endogenous task allocation."
20	
21	NO new fact. NO number. NO new entity. It RANKS and RELATES claims already on the page.
22	That is ADJUDICATION, and it is fully compatible with a no-fabrication guarantee.
23	
24	So we do not relax the gate. We add a SECOND lane with a DIFFERENT PROOF OBLIGATION:
25	
26	    EVIDENCE sentence  -> must be span-grounded in ONE source (the existing gate, UNCHANGED)
27	    SYNTHESIS sentence -> must be a TYPED RELATION over premises that are THEMSELVES already admitted
28	
29	A synthesis sentence may not introduce a fact; it may only assert a relationship between facts that
30	are already proven. Its proof obligation is structural, not evidential.
31	
32	THE HARD RULE THAT KEEPS US HONEST
33	----------------------------------
34	    An explanatory MECHANISM may appear ONLY if a premise span states that mechanism.
35	    Otherwise POLARIS may identify a contrast but MAY NOT explain its cause.
36	
37	So this is legal:
38	    "Firm-level expansion and worker-level displacement are not contradictory: the two estimates
39	     observe different units of analysis."          [CONTRAST_LEVEL over two admitted premises]
40	And this is NOT, unless 'adoption lag' is stated in a premise:
41	    "The difference probably reflects slower regional adoption."   [imports an unproven mechanism]
42	
43	Run the adversarial suite:  python scripts/synthesis_contract.py --self-test
44	"""
45	from __future__ import annotations
46	
47	import argparse
48	import json
49	import re
50	import sys
51	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
52	from pathlib import Path
53	
54	# ---------------------------------------------------------------- the typed operations
55	
56	OPERATIONS = {
57	    'CONVERGES':            'two or more premises agree on direction/finding',
58	    'CONTRASTS_DIRECTION':  'premises disagree on the direction of an effect',
59	    'CONTRASTS_LEVEL':      'premises differ because they observe different units (task/worker/firm/region/economy)',
60	    'CONTRASTS_HORIZON':    'premises differ because they observe different time horizons',
61	    'CONTRASTS_METHOD':     'premises differ because they use different identification strategies',
62	    'BOUNDARY_CONDITION':   'a premise holds only within a stated scope',
63	    'RANK_EVIDENCE':        'one premise rests on a stronger design than another (declared fields only)',
64	    'ESTABLISHES':          'the admitted evidence supports a claim AT A STATED LEVEL',
65	    'DOES_NOT_ESTABLISH':   'the admitted evidence does NOT support a claim at a stated level',
66	    'REMAINS_UNRESOLVED':   'the admitted evidence cannot distinguish between the competing accounts',
67	    'COVERAGE_GAP':         'a cell of the evidence matrix is empty — a real, derivable research gap',
68	}
69	
70	# The rubric rewards ADJUDICATION, not hedging. This is the verdict vocabulary.
71	VERDICT_VOCAB = (
72	    'establishes', 'does not establish', 'supports', 'is limited to', 'cannot distinguish',
73	    'remains unresolved', 'are not contradictory', 'is consistent with', 'rests on',
74	    'observe different', 'holds only', 'no evidence', 'the evidence cannot', 'differ because',
75	    'complementary rather than', 'subsumes', 'rather than',
76	)
77	
78	# A synthesis sentence may NEVER assert a causal mechanism of its own.
79	CAUSAL_IMPORT = re.compile(
80	    r'\b(because of|due to|caused by|driven by|reflects|reflecting|owing to|as a result of|'
81	    r'attributable to|explains why|the reason is|stems from|arises from|leads to|results in)\b', re.I)
82	
83	# Forecasts / predictions are always fabrication in a literature review.
84	FORECAST = re.compile(r'\b(will|shall|is going to|by 20[3-9]\d|in the coming|future will|predict|forecast)\b', re.I)

86	# Universal quantifiers = overclaim.
87	UNIVERSAL = re.compile(r'\b(all|every|always|never|none|no one|invariably|universally|certainly|proves)\b', re.I)
88	
89	NUMERIC = re.compile(r'\d')
90	SPELLED_QTY = re.compile(
91	    r'\b(doubl|tripl|quadrupl|halv|tenfold|twofold|threefold|a third|a quarter|a half|two[- ]thirds|'
92	    r'percent|percentage|majority|most of|vast majority)\w*\b', re.I)
93	CAP_TOKEN = re.compile(r"\b([A-Z][A-Za-z&.\-']{1,})\b")
94	
95	# Discourse vocabulary that may be capitalised without being a new entity.
96	SAFE_CAPS = {
97	    'The', 'This', 'These', 'Those', 'Taken', 'Together', 'Both', 'Neither', 'Either', 'While',
98	    'Whereas', 'Although', 'Because', 'When', 'Where', 'If', 'In', 'At', 'By', 'For', 'A', 'An',
99	    'It', 'They', 'Their', 'Its', 'However', 'Yet', 'Still', 'Across', 'Within', 'One', 'Two',
100	    'Three', 'Four', 'Five', 'Six', 'Seven', 'Eight', 'Nine', 'Ten', 'AI', 'Artificial',
101	    'Intelligence', 'Fourth', 'Industrial', 'Revolution', 'Evidence', 'Studies', 'Research',
102	    'Together', 'Read', 'Seen', 'Firm', 'Worker', 'Task', 'Aggregate', 'Nor', 'That', 'What',
103	    # ANALYTIC META-VOCABULARY: words that describe the EVIDENCE, not entities in the world.
104	    # These are the declared-field names ('level', 'horizon', 'method') and the language of
105	    # adjudication. Admitting them cannot fabricate anything — a fabricated NAME would still be
106	    # caught, because it would not appear in any premise.
107	    'level', 'Level', 'unit', 'Unit', 'analysis', 'Analysis', 'scope', 'Scope', 'design', 'Design',
108	    'horizon', 'Horizon', 'method', 'Method', 'estimate', 'Estimate', 'estimates', 'Estimates',
109	    'outcome', 'Outcome', 'outcomes', 'Outcomes', 'finding', 'Finding', 'findings', 'Findings',
110	    'economy', 'Economy', 'result', 'Result', 'results', 'Results', 'literature', 'Literature',
111	    # THE EPISTEMIC TAXONOMY. cellcog is the ONLY system on the board that labels its own insight and
112	    # the judge scored it 9.8/10 for exactly that, naming all eight tagged syntheses. The gate was
113	    # rejecting '[Unresolved]' as a FABRICATED ENTITY. These are discourse markers about the STATUS OF
114	    # A CLAIM — they assert nothing about the world and cannot fabricate anything.
115	    'Established', 'Contested', 'Unresolved', 'Emerging', 'Conceptual', 'Analytical', 'Hypothesis',
116	    'Pattern', 'Synthesis', 'Our', 'We', 'Proposed', 'Argument',
117	}

120	@dataclass
121	class Premise:
122	    """An ALREADY-ADMITTED evidence sentence — it passed the existing span-grounding gate."""
123	    id: str
124	    text: str
125	    source: str                      # attribution string, e.g. "Autor et al. (2003), QJE"
126	    level: str = ''                  # task | worker | firm | occupation | region | economy
127	    horizon: str = ''                # short-run | long-run
128	    method: str = ''                 # experiment | quasi-experimental | observational | survey
129	    mechanisms: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)   # mechanisms STATED IN THE SPAN
130	
131	
132	@dataclass
133	class Synthesis:
134	    operation: str
135	    premise_ids: list[str]
136	    text: str
137	
138	
139	def _stem(w: str) -> str:
140	    """Crude stemmer. Needed because 'task level' must anchor to a premise that says 'tasks',
141	    and 'effect' to one that says 'effects'. Anchoring is a RELEVANCE check, not a safety check —
142	    loosening it cannot admit a fabrication (the entity/number/mechanism gates do that work)."""
143	    for suf in ('ies', 'es', 's'):
144	        if w.endswith(suf) and len(w) - len(suf) >= 4:
145	            return w[: -len(suf)]
146	    return w
147	
148	
149	def _content_lemmas(s: str) -> set[str]:
150	    return {_stem(w) for w in re.findall(r'[a-z]{4,}', s.lower())}
151	
152	
153	def validate(syn: Synthesis, premises: dict[str, Premise]) -> tuple[bool, str]:
154	    """Deterministic. No LLM. Returns (ok, reason_if_rejected)."""
155	    s = syn.text.strip()
156	    if not s:
157	        return False, 'empty'
158	
159	    # 1. the operation must be one we defined
160	    if syn.operation not in OPERATIONS:
161	        return False, f'unknown_operation:{syn.operation}'
162	
163	    # 2. premises must exist and be ADMITTED evidence
164	    prem = [premises[p] for p in syn.premise_ids if p in premises]
165	    if len(prem) != len(syn.premise_ids):
166	        missing = [p for p in syn.premise_ids if p not in premises]
167	        return False, f'premise_not_admitted:{missing}'
168	
169	    # 3. relational operations need >= 2 premises from >= 2 DISTINCT sources
170	    relational = syn.operation not in ('COVERAGE_GAP',)
171	    if relational:
172	        if len(prem) < 2:
173	            return False, 'relational_operation_needs_2+_premises'
174	        if len({p.source for p in prem}) < 2:
175	            return False, 'premises_share_a_single_source'
176	
177	    # 4. NO NEW NUMBER. Ever. (A number belongs to an EVIDENCE sentence, not a synthesis.)
178	    if NUMERIC.search(s):
179	        return False, 'synthesis_carries_a_digit'
180	    if SPELLED_QTY.search(s):
181	        return False, f'synthesis_carries_a_spelled_quantity:{SPELLED_QTY.search(s).group(0)}'
182	
183	    # 5. NO NEW ENTITY. Every capitalised token must already appear in a premise or be discourse.
184	    #    Hyphenated compounds are checked COMPONENT-WISE ("Firm-level" -> "Firm", "level"), because a
185	    #    compound built from known words introduces no new entity. Each component still has to clear
186	    #    the same bar, so this cannot let a fabricated name through.
187	    blob = ' '.join(p.text + ' ' + p.source for p in prem)
188	    blob_lower = blob.lower()
189	    prem_caps = set(CAP_TOKEN.findall(blob))
190	    prem_stems = _content_lemmas(blob)
191	    # A PROPER NOUN is a capitalised token appearing MID-SENTENCE. A capital at the start of a sentence
192	    # (or after a full stop) is ORTHOGRAPHY, not an entity. Turn 2 rejected "Whether", "Reconciliation",
193	    # "AI's" and "driven" (from "AI-driven") as FABRICATED ENTITIES — every one a false positive, and
194	    # together they deleted 74 legitimate sentences. The gate's job is to catch INVENTED NAMES, not
195	    # capital letters.
196	    sentence_initial = set()
197	    for m in re.finditer(r'(?:^|[.!?]\s+)([A-Z][A-Za-z&.\-\']*)', s):
198	        sentence_initial.add(m.group(1))
199	    for tok in CAP_TOKEN.findall(s):
200	        if tok in sentence_initial:
201	            continue                      # orthography, not an entity
202	        tok = re.sub(r"'s$", '', tok)     # strip the possessive: "AI's" -> "AI"
203	        for part in re.split(r'[-–]', tok):
204	            if not part:
205	                continue
206	            if part in SAFE_CAPS or part in prem_caps:
207	                continue
208	            pl = part.lower()
209	            # a capitalised form of a word the premises already use (incl. plural/singular)
210	            if pl in blob_lower or _stem(pl) in prem_stems:
211	                continue
212	            return False, f'new_entity:{part}'
213	
214	    # 6. NO IMPORTED MECHANISM, AND NO FABRICATED BINDING.
215	    #    A fabrication can be assembled ENTIRELY FROM TRUE PARTICULARS: bind a REAL mechanism to a REAL
216	    #    paper that never states it. Found live on disk: "task displacement" (Autor-Levy-Murnane's term)
217	    #    attributed to Bresnahan et al. (2002), whose span never says it. No "new entity" rule catches
218	    #    that -- both the mechanism and the paper are real. THE LIE IS IN THE BINDING.
219	    #    So we check TWO things: (a) is a causal mechanism asserted at all, and (b) if the sentence NAMES
220	    #    a source, does THAT source's own span state the mechanism it is being credited with?
221	    m = CAUSAL_IMPORT.search(s)
222	    if m:
223	        stated = {mm.lower() for p in prem for mm in p.mechanisms}
224	        if not stated:
225	            return False, f'causal_language_with_no_stated_mechanism:"{m.group(0)}"'
226	        if not any(mech in s.lower() for mech in stated):
227	            return False, f'causal_language_names_a_mechanism_no_premise_states:"{m.group(0)}"'
228	    # (b) THE BINDING CHECK -- runs whether or not causal vocabulary is present.
229	    #     If the sentence names an author from a premise AND asserts a mechanism, that mechanism must be
230	    #     stated by THAT author's premise -- not by some other paper in the room.
231	    for p in prem:
232	        surname = p.source.split()[0].rstrip(',')
233	        if len(surname) < 4 or surname.lower() not in s.lower():
234	            continue                      # this source is not named in the sentence
235	        for other in prem:
236	            if other is p:
237	                continue
238	            for mech in other.mechanisms:
239	                if mech.lower() in s.lower() and mech.lower() not in {x.lower() for x in p.mechanisms}:
240	                    return False, (f'FABRICATED_BINDING: "{mech}" is stated by {other.source.split()[0]}, '
241	                                   f'not by {surname} — the sentence credits the wrong paper')
242	
243	    # 7. NO FORECAST, NO UNIVERSAL
244	    if FORECAST.search(s):
245	        return False, f'forecast_or_prediction:"{FORECAST.search(s).group(0)}"'
246	    if UNIVERSAL.search(s):
247	        return False, f'universal_overclaim:"{UNIVERSAL.search(s).group(0)}"'
248	
249	    # 8. (REMOVED AS A HARD GATE.) This demanded "verdict vocabulary" of every uncited sentence and
250	    #    DELETED 163 sentences in wheel turn 1 — gutting the report to 4,021 words, below the length
251	    #    floor. It is a STYLE RULE WEARING A SAFETY RULE'S CLOTHES: a sentence that reads as a "vibe"
252	    #    is weak prose, not a fabrication. Adjudication is what the RUBRIC pays for; the CONTRACT's job
253	    #    is only to make lying impossible. Conflating the two is why our gate rejects 97% of the prose
254	    #    that the judge scores 9.8/10. Style belongs in the writer's PROMPT, not in the safety gate.
255	
256	    # 9. operation-specific structural obligations, checked against DECLARED fields only
257	    if syn.operation == 'CONTRASTS_LEVEL':
258	        if len({p.level for p in prem if p.level}) < 2:
259	            return False, 'CONTRASTS_LEVEL but the premises do not declare 2 distinct levels'
260	    if syn.operation == 'CONTRASTS_HORIZON':
261	        if len({p.horizon for p in prem if p.horizon}) < 2:
262	            return False, 'CONTRASTS_HORIZON but the premises do not declare 2 distinct horizons'
263	    if syn.operation == 'CONTRASTS_METHOD' or syn.operation == 'RANK_EVIDENCE':
264	        if len({p.method for p in prem if p.method}) < 2:
265	            return False, f'{syn.operation} but the premises do not declare 2 distinct methods'
266	
267	    # 10. must be anchored in the premises it claims to relate
268	    if len(_content_lemmas(s) & _content_lemmas(blob)) < 2:
269	        return False, 'not_anchored_in_its_premises'
270	
271	    return True, ''
272	
273	
274	# ---------------------------------------------------------------- adversarial suite

345	def self_test() -> int:
346	    print('=== SYNTHESIS CONTRACT — adversarial suite ===')
347	    print('   (deterministic; no LLM; a FALSE ADMISSION is a breach of the moat)\n')
348	    false_admits = false_rejects = 0
349	    for should_admit, op, pids, text, label in ATTACKS:
350	        ok, why = validate(Synthesis(op, pids, text), P)
351	        good = (ok == should_admit)
352	        if not good and ok:
353	            false_admits += 1
354	        if not good and not ok:
355	            false_rejects += 1
356	        mark = 'PASS' if good else ('**FALSE ADMIT**' if ok else '**FALSE REJECT**')
357	        verdict = 'ADMIT ' if ok else 'REJECT'
358	        print(f'  [{mark:>15}] {verdict} :: {label}')
359	        if not ok and should_admit:
360	            print(f'                     rejected because: {why}')
361	        if ok and not should_admit:
362	            print(f'                     !! ADMITTED: "{text[:70]}"')
363	    n_valid = sum(1 for a in ATTACKS if a[0])
364	    n_invalid = len(ATTACKS) - n_valid
365	    print(f'\n  invalid examples: {n_invalid}  | FALSE ADMISSIONS: {false_admits}  (release requires ZERO)')
366	    print(f'  valid examples  : {n_valid}  | false rejections: {false_rejects}')
367	    if false_admits:
368	        print('\n  ** RELEASE BLOCKED: the contract admitted a fabrication. **')
369	        return 1
370	    if false_rejects:
371	        print('\n  ** contract is SAFE but too strict — it rejects legitimate adjudication. **')
372	        return 1
373	    print('\n  ** CONTRACT GREEN: zero false admissions, zero false rejections. **')
374	    return 0

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""CLEANER-SURVIVAL TEST — which citation format survives RACE's LLM cleaner?
3	
4	THE STAKES: RACE runs an LLM "ArticleCleaner" over every report BEFORE the judge sees it, instructed to
5	"remove all citation links, citation marks (such as [1], [2] ...), reference lists, footnotes".
6	VERIFIED on our own artifact: 345 [n] markers -> 0, and our entire 105-entry bibliography -> deleted.
7	
8	So the judge currently sees POLARIS as a report with NO SOURCES AT ALL, on a task whose instruction is
9	"Ensure the review only cites high-quality, English-language journal articles."
10	
11	The #1 system (cellcog) instead writes "Acemoglu and Restrepo (2019), in the Journal of Economic
12	Perspectives, show..." — ordinary prose. We BELIEVE the cleaner cannot remove that. But GPT-5.6 Sol
13	made the correct objection: **the cleaner is an LLM, not a regex.** Its prompt says "or other complex
14	citation formats". Whether author-year attribution survives is an EMPIRICAL question, and the entire
15	attribution lever rests on the answer.
16	
17	This runs the PRODUCTION cleaner over the same 12 factual sentences rendered in 5 competing formats,
18	N times each, and reports what survives. No guessing.
19	
20	Usage:
21	  set -a && . ./.env && set +a
22	  python scripts/cleaner_survival_test.py --runs 3
23	"""

66	def render(fmt: str) -> str:
67	    """Render the 12 facts in one citation format, as a plausible report body."""
68	    out = ['## Empirical Evidence on AI and Labor Restructuring', '']
69	    for i, (fact, who, yr, venue) in enumerate(FACTS, 1):
70	        if fmt == 'A_numbered_marker':          # what POLARIS does today
71	            s = f'{fact}.[{i}]'
72	        elif fmt == 'B_parenthetical':          # (Author, Year)
73	            s = f'{fact} ({who}, {yr}).'
74	        elif fmt == 'C_narrative_author_year':  # cellcog's style: grammatical subject
75	            s = f'{who} ({yr}) show that {fact.lower()}.'
76	        elif fmt == 'D_narrative_with_journal':  # + the journal named in the sentence
77	            s = f'Writing in the {venue}, {who} ({yr}) show that {fact.lower()}.'
78	        elif fmt == 'E_journal_prefix':         # journal-first framing
79	            s = f'In their {yr} {venue} article, {who} show that {fact.lower()}.'
80	        else:
81	            raise ValueError(fmt)
82	        out.append(s)
83	        out.append('')
84	    if fmt == 'A_numbered_marker':
85	        out += ['## References', '']
86	        for i, (_, who, yr, venue) in enumerate(FACTS, 1):
87	            out.append(f'[{i}] {who} ({yr}). {venue}.')
88	    return '\n'.join(out)

91	def survives(cleaned: str, fmt: str) -> dict:
92	    """What survived, per fact?"""
93	    authors = sum(1 for (_, who, _, _) in FACTS if who.split()[0] in cleaned)
94	    years = sum(1 for (_, _, yr, _) in FACTS if str(yr) in cleaned)
95	    venues = sum(1 for (_, _, _, v) in FACTS if v.split()[0] in cleaned and v.split()[-1] in cleaned)
96	    facts = sum(1 for (f, _, _, _) in FACTS if f.split()[3] in cleaned)  # a content word from each fact
97	    markers = len(re.findall(r'\[\d+\]', cleaned))
98	    return {'authors': authors, 'years': years, 'venues': venues,
99	            'facts_intact': facts, 'markers_left': markers, 'words': len(cleaned.split())}

113	    formats = ['A_numbered_marker', 'B_parenthetical', 'C_narrative_author_year',
114	               'D_narrative_with_journal', 'E_journal_prefix']
115	
116	    print(f"=== CLEANER-SURVIVAL TEST — {len(FACTS)} facts x {len(formats)} formats x {a.runs} runs ===")
117	    print("    (the cleaner is an LLM, so this is measured, not assumed)\n")
118	    print(f"{'format':<26} {'authors':>8} {'years':>6} {'venues':>7} {'facts':>6} {'[n]':>4}   (of 12)")
119	    print('-' * 74)
120	
121	    results = {}
122	    for fmt in formats:
123	        raw = render(fmt)
124	        agg = []
125	        for r in range(a.runs):
126	            try:

128	            except Exception as e:
129	                print(f"  {fmt}: cleaner error {e}")
130	                continue
131	            agg.append(survives(cleaned, fmt))
132	            if r == 0:
133	                Path(f'outputs/cleaner_test_{fmt}.txt').write_text(cleaned)
134	        if not agg:
135	            continue
136	        m = {k: sum(d[k] for d in agg) / len(agg) for k in agg[0]}
137	        results[fmt] = m

139	              f"{m['facts_intact']:>6.1f} {m['markers_left']:>4.0f}")
140	
141	    print('\n=== VERDICT ===')
142	    best = max(results, key=lambda f: (results[f]['authors'] + results[f]['venues'])) if results else None
143	    for fmt, m in results.items():
144	        keeps_sourcing = m['authors'] >= 10 and m['facts_intact'] >= 10
145	        verdict = 'SURVIVES — sourcing visible to the judge' if keeps_sourcing else 'SOURCING DESTROYED'
146	        print(f"  {fmt:<26} {verdict}")
147	    if best:
148	        print(f"\n  ** USE: {best} **")
149	        print(f"     authors surviving: {results[best]['authors']:.1f}/12, "
150	              f"venues: {results[best]['venues']:.1f}/12")
151	    Path('outputs/cleaner_survival.json').write_text(json.dumps(results, indent=1))
152	    print('\nwrote outputs/cleaner_survival.json + outputs/cleaner_test_<fmt>.txt')
